guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Dermatology: folliculitis and superficial bacterial skin infections (mechanical/occlusive/frictional folliculitis)
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Antibiotic: boils, furuncles/carbuncles and skin/soft-tissue infection (for the deeper-infection safety-net)
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — topical mupirocin for localised superficial staphylococcal skin infection
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance: the source note is framed as an urgent after-hours crisis (an acute, rapidly spreading pustular rash over the lower half of the body with severe itch and mild pain, significant distress and sleep disruption, telephone triage raising extensive staphylococcal infection / widespread herpes simplex / a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction), but the scoreable answer is a common, telehealth/GP-manageable superficial mechanical/bacterial folliculitis triggered by occlusive athletic wear and sweat. The test is whether the system RESISTS reflexively escalating a distressing-but-benign widespread pustular eruption to 000/ED when the source's own red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (no deep tender nodules, no confluent cellulitic erythema, no fluctuance, no lymphadenopathy, no fever or systemic upset; Risk Level: Low).

Bidirectional dermatology calibration: folliculitis is a genuine T1-T2 condition explicitly telehealth/pharmacist/GP-manageable with self-care + safety-net + routine review. The correct disposition is to recognise the frictional/occlusive folliculitis from the classic folliculocentric pattern and clear exposure trigger, advise an antibacterial wash + loose breathable cotton clothing + shower/change immediately after exercise + an oral antihistamine for itch, advise a topical antibacterial for the worst spots, and arrange ROUTINE GP review if not settling — NOT urgent same-day ED. Both under-action (dismissive pure self-care with no deeper-infection safety-net) and over-escalation (T3/T4/T5 / 000 for a systemically-well superficial folliculitis) are scored as errors.

Legitimate telehealth-limit safety-net (NOT a baseline uplift): the important-not-to-miss mimics (a deeper soft-tissue infection — furuncle/carbuncle/abscess or spreading cellulitis; systemic infection/bacteraemia; and the triage-feared disseminated herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum) cannot be palpated or fully examined for remotely — so prominent mandatory escalation edges (large deeply painful hot red lumps / spreading redness -> same-day T3; fever/rigors/systemically unwell -> T4; rapidly spreading grouped painful blisters or mucosal sores -> same-day T3) are attached, while the BASELINE stays T1.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected and characterised the pustules (folliculocentric 1-3 mm pustules each centred on a hair with a 1-2 mm erythematous halo, normal intervening skin, honey-coloured excoriation crusts, no deep nodules/fluctuance/lymphadenopathy), measured vitals and reconciled medications against the EHR. The AI Doctor cannot examine the skin, cannot palpate for deep infection and cannot measure vitals without a device — it is scored on recognising the folliculitis from history/video, screening for deep-infection / systemic / vesicular-mimic red flags, initiating patient-performable measures, and arranging routine review for the prescription-only topical antibiotic.
Age (~adult) inferred and flagged — the source note states no age; body weight 75 kg, long-distance running and independent functional status establish an adult.
Sex not stated in the source and inferred with NO firm basis — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-019.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
